behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is local backoff math/state progression (attempt count -> delay with cap), with no required module handoff, I/O, or real clock/OS dependency. A pure function/object test can catch real failures (wrong doubling, overflow, cap not applied). Higher levels were considered and rejected because the behavior text is algorithmic only and does not mention transport/timer/queue interactions.
behaviors.test_description: Test the backoff policy function/object directly: feed consecutive failure counts and assert delays are 1x, 2x, 4x... until max, then remain capped; include edge cases around max boundary.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears at a module boundary: HTTP response classification + retry scheduler/backoff computation must work together. Unit tests of either piece alone can miss contract mismatches (e.g., status/header parsing handed off incorrectly). System was rejected because behavior text only requires interpreting response data and computing wait, not real network/OS effects.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry middleware/service with a stub transport returning a synthetic rate-limit response (e.g., 429 plus retry metadata) and assert it is classified as retryable and next-delay value is computed/applied from that response.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Needs orchestration across retry state, timer/backoff, and request executor to verify resend uses original request and successful response is surfaced unchanged. Mocking all internals at unit scope can hide handoff bugs. System rejected because real HTTP/subprocess is not required; in-process stub transport plus controlled clock reproduces the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers and a stub transport that returns rate-limit once then success; call `call()` once, advance time by computed delay, assert transport was invoked twice with equivalent request payload and final promise resolves to the success result.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is in retry-loop termination plus error-mapping boundary (retry controller -> public API). A unit test of error class alone is insufficient; need wired retry path to prove final surfaced type. System rejected because no real network/OS behavior is needed; deterministic stub failures are enough.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries N, stub transport to always return rate-limit, invoke `call()`, advance timers through N retries, and assert rejection is specifically `QuotaExhaustedError` (not generic/transport error) with expected metadata.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly spans multiple local modules (retry chain + quota tracking + termination condition). This is beyond single-function logic and needs wired state transitions. System rejected because indefinite-loop risk here is from in-process state/logic, reproducible with stub transport and fake timers (no real OS/network required).
behaviors.test_description: Run the full client stack with stubbed persistent rate-limit responses and fake clock; assert attempts count equals configured initial+retries, total scheduled waits match policy, promise settles with terminal error, and no further retries are scheduled afterward.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is shared-state coordination across concurrent callers and quota manager. Unit tests of individual methods can miss cross-module race/ownership bugs. System rejected by explicit text "in the same process"; no real external infra is required.
behaviors.test_description: Create one client instance with shared quota manager, fire multiple concurrent `call()` invocations via stub transport, and assert aggregate consumption is enforced globally (not per-caller), including correct throttling/retry behavior under contention.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Requires queueing/dispatcher + caller-facing API interaction under contention; ordering bugs typically emerge at module handoff and async scheduling boundaries, not in isolated function checks. System rejected because ordering can be validated with in-process queue and controlled timers without real network/OS primitives.
behaviors.test_description: Submit several calls quickly while quota forces waiting; use deterministic scheduler/fake timers and stub transport to record send order; assert dispatch order matches submission order (FIFO) across retry/wait transitions.
